To earn 1 point

To earn 1 point, submit all jamovi OMV files in this Computer lab that contain the activities you completed here. The subject of the email should be Stat3 - Comp. lab 10. Send the email to abari.kurzus@gmail.com.

Worked Example 1

The management of a company wanted to examine the impact of different management programs, so it introduced three different management programs in three different strategic business units (SBU). The program introduced in

  • the first SBU emphasized equality and individualism
  • the second SBU, equality and teamwork were emphasized
  • the third BSU applied a very hierarchical management principle.

Later, a survey was conducted among employees in all three BUs, and the questions included

  • the degree of commitment to the organization (committed),
  • the level of satisfaction with the organization (satisfied),
  • and the egalitarian or authoritarian (autocratic) nature of the system (system).

Let us examine whether the three groups of SBU can be considered identical in terms of their answers to the three questions (committed, satisfied, and system).

We already examine the differences between the groups in the previous lab, but now we will use Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) to examine the differences between the groups. The LDA is a supervised classification method that can be used to find a linear combination of features that separates two or more classes of objects or events. MANOVA already examined the differences between the groups, but LDA can be used to find the linear combination of features that best separates the groups. In this case, we will use LDA to find the linear combination of committed, satisfied, and system that best separates the three groups of SBU. In this sense, LDA is the post-hoc test of MANOVA.

The data is available in the file management_programs.xlsx.

Step 1. Import database

Open the management_programs.xlsx file in jamovi.

Step 2. Measure type

Set the measurement level of the variables. The committed, satisfied and system variables are continuous, so we set the measurement level to Continuous. The SBU variable is categorical, so we set the measurement level to Nominal.

Step 3. Installing snowCluster plugin

We need to install the snowCluster module to perform LDA in jamovi. This module is not included in the default jamovi installation, so we need to install it separately.

To do this, go to the Modules icon in the top menu and select jamovi library.

In the search box, type snowCluster and click on the Install button. If it has already been installed, you can skip this step. If you would see the Update button instead of the Install button, click on it to update the module.

After the installation is complete, you will see the snowCluster icon in the top menu. Click on it to open the module.

We will use 3 menu Linear Discriminant Analysis in the snowCluster module.

Step 4. Linear Discriminant Analysis

To perform LDA, click on the snowCluster icon in the top menu and select Linear Discriminant Analysis. Drag the SBU variable to the Dependent box, and the committed, satisfied, and system variables to the Covariates box. The SBU variable is the dependent variable, while the committed, satisfied, and system variables are the independent variables.

Then check the Coefficients and Classification options. The Coefficients option will show the coefficients of the linear discriminant function, while the Classification option will show the classification results.

Step 5. Interpretation of the results

Prior Probabilities

Group Probability
1 0.3333
2 0.3333
3 0.3333

Interpretation:

  • Each group was assumed to have equal prior probability (1/3 chance) because the groups were likely either naturally balanced or intentionally set to be treated equally.

Coefficients of Linear Discriminants

Variable LD1 LD2
committed –0.0706 0.4959
satisfied 0.1019 0.8596
system –1.3236 0.5392

How to read this: - LD1 (Linear Discriminant 1) and LD2 (Linear Discriminant 2) are two new axes created by the LDA to best separate the groups. - The numbers are coefficients showing how strongly each original variable contributes to each discriminant axis.

Key points: - LD1 is mainly driven by system (coefficient = –1.3236), meaning system is the most important variable for separating groups along LD1. - LD2 is mainly influenced by satisfied (coefficient = 0.8596) and moderately by committed and system.

As we know, the number of discriminant functions is equal to minimum of two values: the number of groups minus one and the number of variables. In this case, we have three groups and three variables, so we can have two discriminant functions.

Linear Discriminant Plot

  • Each dot represents a participant plotted on the two discriminant functions (LD1 and LD2).
  • Different shapes and colors represent the three SBU groups:
    • Group 1 (triangle, orange)
    • Group 2 (upward triangle, dark gray)
    • Group 3 (circle, blue)
  • Ellipses show the spread (variability) of each group.
  • Centroids (big triangle/circle in the middle of each group) show the average position of the group on the LD axes.

Interpretation: - Group 1 (orange) is well separated from Groups 2 and 3, mostly on LD1. - Groups 2 and 3 overlap slightly more but are separated somewhat along LD2. - ➔ The model does a good job distinguishing Group 1, but Groups 2 and 3 may be a little harder to tell apart completely.

Quick Summary

  • LDA found two discriminant functions (LD1, LD2) that best separate the SBUs.
  • System is the most important variable for separating groups along LD1.
  • Satisfied is the most important variable for separating groups along LD2.
  • Group 1 is clearly separated, Groups 2 and 3 show moderate overlap.

Final Thought

  • LDA shows that the SBUs differ meaningfully based on system satisfaction and satisfied ratings.
  • Group 1 can be predicted with high accuracy, while distinguishing between Groups 2 and 3 would be harder.

Futher analysis

We can check another options: Proportion of Trace and Confusion Matrix.

Proportion of Trace

Discriminant Function Proportion (%)
LD1 96.16%
LD2 3.84%

Meaning:

  • LD1 (First Discriminant Function) explains 96.16% of the total variance between groups.
  • LD2 (Second Discriminant Function) explains only 3.84%.

Interpretation:

  • Almost all of the separation between the groups is captured by LD1.
  • LD2 adds very little extra separation.
  • In practice, you can mostly focus on LD1 when interpreting group differences.

Confusion Matrix with Training Set

True Group Predicted as Group 1 Predicted as Group 2 Predicted as Group 3
Group 1 4 3 3
Group 2 1 9 0
Group 3 0 0 10

How to read it:

  • Rows = true group membership.
  • Columns = predicted group membership from the LDA model.

Detailed breakdown:

  • Group 1:
    • 4 correctly classified as Group 1.
    • 3 misclassified as Group 2.
    • 3 misclassified as Group 3.
  • Group 2:
    • 9 correctly classified as Group 2.
    • 1 misclassified as Group 1.
  • Group 3:
    • 10 correctly classified as Group 3 (perfect classification).

Overall Model Performance

Good news:

  • Group 3 is classified perfectly (10 out of 10 correctly predicted).
  • Group 2 is classified very well (9 correct, only 1 error).

Problematic area:

  • Group 1 has poorer classification:
  • Only 4 out of 10 are correctly predicted.
  • 6 participants from Group 1 are misclassified into Groups 2 and 3.

General Interpretation:

  • The model separates Group 2 and Group 3 very well.
  • Group 1 is harder to predict accurately — possibly due to overlap in the covariate values (committed, satisfied, system).
